blocks.html: VIII Faithfully flat descent

pages.title: Descent of quasi-coherent Modules
pages.html:
1 Descent of quasi-coherent Modules

Let \(\mathbf{Sch}\) be the category of preschemes. Proceeding as in VI 11.b, one finds that the category of pairs \((X,F)\) consisting of a prescheme \(X\) and a Module \(F\) on \(X\), (where the morphisms are defined as in loc. cit. by means of the notion of direct image of a Module under a morphism of ringed spaces) may be regarded as a category fibred over \(\mathbf{Sch}\), the base-change functor relative to a morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathbf{Sch}\) being the inverse-image functor of Modules under \(f\). (One will note that the fibre category at \(X\in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{Sch})\) of the preceding fibred category is the category opposite to the category of Modules on \(X\)). Since the inverse image of a quasi-coherent Module is quasi-coherent, one sees that the full subcategory of the category of pairs \((X,F)\), formed of those pairs for which \(F\) is quasi-coherent, is a fibred subcategory of the preceding fibred category. (On the other hand, if no hypotheses are made on \(f\), the direct image of a quasi-coherent Module is not in general a quasi-coherent Module). This fibred category will simply be called the fibred category of quasi-coherent Modules on the preschemes.

Recall on the other hand that a morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of ringed spaces is said to be faithfully flat if it is flat (i.e. for every \(x\in X\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is a flat module over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,f(x)}\), (IV)), and surjective. One says that \(f\) is a quasi-compact morphism if the inverse image under \(f\) of every quasi-compact part is quasi-compact; when \(f\) is a morphism of preschemes, this also means that the inverse image under \(f\) of an affine open of \(Y\) is a finite union of affine opens of \(X\).

Theorem 1

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be the fibred category of quasi-coherent Modules on the preschemes. Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes, faithfully flat and quasi-compact. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent.

Recall1 that this means two things:

Corollary 2 (Descent of homomorphisms of Modules)

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes, faithfully flat and quasi-compact, \(F\) and \(G\) two quasi-coherent Modules on \(S\), \(F'\) and \(G'\) their inverse images on \(S'\), and finally \(F''\) and \(G''\) their inverse images on \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\). Consider the diagram of maps of sets defined by the base-change functors by \(g\), \(p_{1}\), \(p_{2}\) (where \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{p_{1},p_{2}\colon S'\times_{S}S'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}}\) are the two projections):

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_{S}(F,G)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(F',G')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(F'',G'').}} \]

This diagram is exact, i.e. defines a bijection of the first set onto the set of coincidences of the two maps written from the second into the third.

In other terms, the base-change functor by \(g\), \(F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F'\), defines a fully faithful functor from the category of quasi-coherent Modules on \(S\) into the category of quasi-coherent Modules on \(S'\) equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\). Moreover:

Corollary 3 (Descent of Modules)

For every quasi-coherent Module \(F'\) on \(S'\), every descent datum on \(F'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, i.e. \(F'\) is isomorphic, together with its descent datum, to the inverse image under \(g\) of a quasi-coherent Module on \(S\) (determined up to unique isomorphism by virtue of 2).

In other terms, the preceding fully faithful functor is even an equivalence. In practice, this means that giving a quasi-coherent Module on \(S\) amounts to the same thing as giving a quasi-coherent Module on \(S'\) equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\).

Proof of 1

Let first \(T\) be an \(S\)-prescheme which is \(S\)-isomorphic to the sum of a family of induced opens \(S_{i}\) of \(S\) which cover \(S\). Then it is obvious that the structural morphism \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent (this means precisely that the datum of a quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on \(S\) is equivalent to the datum of quasi-coherent Modules \(F_{i}\) on the \(S_{i}\), and of gluing isomorphisms \(\varphi_{ji}\colon F_{i}|S_{i}\cap S_{j}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_{j}|S_{i}\cap S_{j}\) satisfying the well-known cochain condition). By virtue of VII, 8 it follows that in order to verify that \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, it suffices to verify it for the morphism \(g_{T}\colon T'=T\times_{S}S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) deduced from \(g\) by the base change \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). (Note that the hypothesis on \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) remains stable under arbitrary base change, hence that \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is in fact a morphism of universal effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent). Taking for \(S_{i}\) affine opens which cover \(S\), one is thus reduced to the case where \(S\) is affine.

Then \(S'\) is a finite union of affine opens, and taking the \(S\)-scheme sum of the latter, one finds an affine \(S\)-scheme \(S_{1}\) and an \(S\)-morphism \(S_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) flat and surjective. Hence \(S_{1}\) is also faithfully flat over \(S\). If then one proves that a faithfully flat and affine morphism is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, hence a morphism of universal strict \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, (the hypothesis being in effect stable under base change), one concludes in particular that the structural morphism \(S_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of universal strict \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, and since there exists an \(S\)-morphism \(S_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), it will indeed follow, by [D], that \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of strict \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent.

.5cmThis thus reduces us to the case where \(g\) is an affine morphism, and as we have seen we may then moreover assume \(S\) affine, hence we may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) affine. In this case, 2 is equivalent to the

Lemma 4

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(A'\) a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra, \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-modules, \(M'\) and \(N'\) the \(A'\)-modules deduced by change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\), and \(M''\),\(N''\) the \(A''=A'\otimes_{A}A'\)-modules deduced by change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A''\). Then the sequence of set-theoretic maps

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_{A}(M,N)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(M',N')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{A''}(M'',N'')}} \]

is exact.

Since the homomorphism \(N\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N'\) is injective (\(A'\) being faithfully flat over \(A\)) one sees that the first arrow is injective. It remains to prove that if an \(A'\)-homomorphism \(u'\colon M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N'\) is compatible with the descent data, then it comes from an \(A\)-homomorphism \(u\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N\). Now this also means simply that \(u'\) maps the subset \(M\) of \(M'\) into the subset \(N\) of \(N'\) (the map \(u\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N\) induced will then automatically be \(A\)-linear since \(u'\) is \(A'\)-linear, and one sees likewise that \(u'\) is necessarily equal to \(u\otimes_{A}A'\)). Now if \(x\in M\), then \(u'(x)\) is an element in the kernel of the pair of maps \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{N'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{N''}}\). One is thus reduced, in order to prove 4, to the following particular case (corresponding to the case where \(M=A\)):

Corollary 5

Let \(N\) be an \(A\)-module; then the sequence of set-theoretic maps

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{N}\ar[r]&{N'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{N''}} \]

is exact.

Indeed, let \(A_{1}\) be a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra. In order to show that the sequence under consideration is exact, it suffices to prove that the sequence deduced from it by the change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_{1}\) is so. Now this latter sequence, as one sees at once, is the one relative to the \(A_1\)-module \(N_{1}=N\otimes_{A}A_{1}\) and to the \(A_{1}\)-algebra \(A_{1}'=A_{1}\otimes_{A}A'\). It therefore suffices to find an \(A_{1}\) faithfully flat over \(A\), such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{1}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A_{1})\) is a strict morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent. Now it suffices indeed to take \(A_{1}=A'\), for then the preceding morphism admits a right inverse morphism, so that by virtue of [D] it is a morphism of effective descent for any fibred category whatsoever over \(\mathbf{Sch}\).

It remains finally to show that if \(N'\) is an \(A'\)-module equipped with a descent datum for \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\), i.e. equipped with an isomorphism

\[ \varphi\colon N_{1}'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} N_{2}' \]

between the two modules deduced from \(N\) by the changes of ring \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{A'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{A'\otimes_{A}A'}}\), then \(N'\) is isomorphic, together with its descent datum, to a module \(N\otimes_{A}A'\). One sees easily, taking into account 5, that this statement is equivalent to the following one:

Lemma 6

Let \(N'\) be an \(A'\)-module equipped with a descent datum relative to \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\) (where \(A'\) is an \(A\)-algebra). Let \(N\) be the sub-\(A\)-module of \(N'\) formed by the \(x\) such that \(\varphi(x\otimes_{A'}1_{A'})=1_{A'}\otimes_{A'}x\), and consider the canonical homomorphism

\[ N\otimes_{A}A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N', \]

(which is then compatible with the descent data). If \(A'\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), this homomorphism is an isomorphism.

Let us prove this lemma. Let again \(A_{1}\) be a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra; in order to show that the morphism under consideration is an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that it becomes one after the change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_1\). Now, using the flatness of \(A_{1}\) over \(A\), one sees that the homomorphism thus obtained is none other than the one that one would obtain directly in terms of the module \(N'\otimes_{A}A_{1}\) over \(A_{1}'=A'\otimes_{A}A_{1}\), equipped with the descent datum relative to \(A_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_{1}'\) that is deduced canonically by change of ring from the one that was given on \(N'\). Thus it suffices to find an \(A_{1}\) faithfully flat over \(A\) such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{1}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A_{1})\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent. One then takes, as above, \(A_{1}=A'\). This completes the proof of 6, and thereby the proof of 1.

Corollary 7 (Descent of sections of Modules)

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes, faithfully flat and quasi-compact. For every quasi-coherent Module \(G\) on \(S\), let \(G'\) and \(G''\) be its inverse images on \(S'\) and \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\), and consider the diagram of homomorphisms of Modules on \(S\):

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{G}\ar[r]&{g_*(G')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(G'')}} \]

(where \(h\colon S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the structural morphism). This diagram is exact.

Indeed, this means that for every open \(U\) in \(S\), the corresponding diagram formed by the sections over \(U\) is exact. One can obviously suppose then that \(U=S\), and the exactness in question is then a particular case of 2, obtained by taking \(F=\mathscr{O}_{S}\).

Since the inverse image functor of Modules is right exact, one concludes formally from 1:

Corollary 8 (Descent of quotient Modules)

With the notation of 7, let moreover, for every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on a prescheme, \(\operatorname{Quot}(F)\) be the set of quasi-coherent quotient Modules of \(F\). With this convention, the diagram of maps of sets:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Quot}(G)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Quot}(G')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Quot}(G'')}} \]

is exact.

(One would obviously have the same statement with the sub-Modules instead of quotient Modules, since the two correspond bijectively). Taking in particular \(G=\mathscr{O}_{S}\), one finds:

Corollary 9 (Descent of closed sub-preschemes)

For every prescheme \(X\), let \(H(X)\) be the set of closed sub-preschemes of \(X\). With this notation, and under the conditions of 7, the following diagram of maps of sets

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{H(S)}\ar[r]&{H(S')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{H(S'')}} \]

is exact.

It is appropriate to complete Theorem 1 by the following result:

Proposition 10 (Descent of properties of Modules)

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(S\). In order for \(F\) to be of finite type, resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free and of finite type, it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse image \(F'\) on \(S'\) be so.

One need only prove the “it suffices”. One can obviously suppose \(S\) affine, and then, replacing \(S'\) by a sum of affine opens covering \(S'\), one is reduced to the case where \(S'\) is likewise affine. Then our statement is equivalent to the following one:

Corollary 11

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(A'\) a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra, \(M\) an \(A\)-module, \(M'\) the \(A'\)-module \(M\otimes_{A}A'\). In order for \(M\) to be of finite type (resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free of finite type) it is necessary and sufficient that \(M'\) be so.

Indeed, we have \(M=\varinjlim_i M_{i}\), where the \(M_{i}\) are the sub-modules of finite type of \(M\). Consequently \(M'=\varinjlim_i M_{i}'\), and if \(M'\) is of finite type, \(M'\) is equal to one of the \(M_{i}'\), so that by faithful flatness \(M\) is equal to \(M_{i}\), whence \(M\) is of finite type. Consequently there exists an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

with \(L\) free of finite type, whence an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

with \(L'\) free of finite type. If then \(M'\) is of finite presentation, \(R'\) is of finite type, so that by what precedes \(R\) is of finite type, whence \(M\) is of finite presentation. Finally, to say that \(M\) is locally free and of finite type means that it is of finite presentation and flat (cf. IV in the Noetherian case; the general case is left to the reader). Since each of these properties descends properly, so does their conjunction, which completes the proof.

Remark 12

The conjunction of 1 and of 10 shows that the statement 1 still remains valid when one replaces the fibred category \(\mathscr{F}\) by the fibred subcategory formed by the Modules that are quasi-coherent of finite type, resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free of finite type, resp. locally free of given rank \(n\).

Lemma 4

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(A'\) a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra, \(M\) and \(N\) two \(A\)-modules, \(M'\) and \(N'\) the \(A'\)-modules deduced by change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\), and \(M''\),\(N''\) the \(A''=A'\otimes_{A}A'\)-modules deduced by change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A''\). \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_{A}(M,N)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(M',N')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{A''}(M'',N'')}} \]

is exact.

It remains to prove that if an \(A'\)-homomorphism \(u'\colon M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N'\) is compatible with the descent data, then it comes from an \(A\)-homomorphism \(u\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N\). blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(N\) be an \(A\)-module; then the sequence of set-theoretic maps

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{N}\ar[r]&{N'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{N''}} \]

is exact.

Indeed, let \(A_{1}\) be a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra. In order to show that the sequence under consideration is exact, it suffices to prove that the sequence deduced from it by the change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_{1}\) is so. Now this latter sequence, as one sees at once, is the one relative to the \(A_1\)-module \(N_{1}=N\otimes_{A}A_{1}\) and to the \(A_{1}\)-algebra \(A_{1}'=A_{1}\otimes_{A}A'\). It therefore suffices to find an \(A_{1}\) faithfully flat over \(A\), such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{1}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A_{1})\) is a strict morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent. Now it suffices indeed to take \(A_{1}=A'\), for then the preceding morphism admits a right inverse morphism, so that by virtue of [D] it is a morphism of effective descent for any fibred category whatsoever over \(\mathbf{Sch}\).

It remains finally to show that if \(N'\) is an \(A'\)-module equipped with a descent datum for \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\), i.e. equipped with an isomorphism
\[ \varphi\colon N_{1}'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} N_{2}' \]

between the two modules deduced from \(N\) by the changes of ring \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{A'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{A'\otimes_{A}A'}}\), then \(N'\) is isomorphic, together with its descent datum, to a module \(N\otimes_{A}A'\). One sees easily, taking into account 5, that this statement is equivalent to the following one:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6

Let \(N'\) be an \(A'\)-module equipped with a descent datum relative to \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\) (where \(A'\) is an \(A\)-algebra). Let \(N\) be the sub-\(A\)-module of \(N'\) formed by the \(x\) such that \(\varphi(x\otimes_{A'}1_{A'})=1_{A'}\otimes_{A'}x\), and consider the canonical homomorphism

\[ N\otimes_{A}A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N', \]

(which is then compatible with the descent data). If \(A'\) is faithfully flat over \(A\), this homomorphism is an isomorphism.

Let us prove this lemma. Let again \(A_{1}\) be a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra; in order to show that the morphism under consideration is an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that it becomes one after the change of ring \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_1\). Now, using the flatness of \(A_{1}\) over \(A\), one sees that the homomorphism thus obtained is none other than the one that one would obtain directly in terms of the module \(N'\otimes_{A}A_{1}\) over \(A_{1}'=A'\otimes_{A}A_{1}\), equipped with the descent datum relative to \(A_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_{1}'\) that is deduced canonically by change of ring from the one that was given on \(N'\). Thus it suffices to find an \(A_{1}\) faithfully flat over \(A\) such that \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{1}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A_{1})\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent. One then takes, as above, \(A_{1}=A'\). This completes the proof of 6, and thereby the proof of 1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7 (Descent of sections of Modules)

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes, faithfully flat and quasi-compact. For every quasi-coherent Module \(G\) on \(S\), let \(G'\) and \(G''\) be its inverse images on \(S'\) and \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\), and consider the diagram of homomorphisms of Modules on \(S\):

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{G}\ar[r]&{g_*(G')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(G'')}} \]

(where \(h\colon S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is the structural morphism). This diagram is exact.

Indeed, this means that for every open \(U\) in \(S\), the corresponding diagram formed by the sections over \(U\) is exact. One can obviously suppose then that \(U=S\), and the exactness in question is then a particular case of 2, obtained by taking \(F=\mathscr{O}_{S}\).
Since the inverse image functor of Modules is right exact, one concludes formally from 1:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8 (Descent of quotient Modules)

With the notation of 7, let moreover, for every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on a prescheme, \(\operatorname{Quot}(F)\) be the set of quasi-coherent quotient Modules of \(F\). With this convention, the diagram of maps of sets:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Quot}(G)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Quot}(G')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Quot}(G'')}} \]

is exact.
(One would obviously have the same statement with the sub-Modules instead of quotient Modules, since the two correspond bijectively). Taking in particular \(G=\mathscr{O}_{S}\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9 (Descent of closed sub-preschemes)

For every prescheme \(X\), let \(H(X)\) be the set of closed sub-preschemes of \(X\). With this notation, and under the conditions of 7, the following diagram of maps of sets

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{H(S)}\ar[r]&{H(S')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{H(S'')}} \]

is exact.
It is appropriate to complete Theorem 1 by the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 10 (Descent of properties of Modules)

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(S\). In order for \(F\) to be of finite type, resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free and of finite type, it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse image \(F'\) on \(S'\) be so.

One need only prove the “it suffices”. One can obviously suppose \(S\) affine, and then, replacing \(S'\) by a sum of affine opens covering \(S'\), one is reduced to the case where \(S'\) is likewise affine. Then our statement is equivalent to the following one:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(A'\) a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra, \(M\) an \(A\)-module, \(M'\) the \(A'\)-module \(M\otimes_{A}A'\). In order for \(M\) to be of finite type (resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free of finite type) it is necessary and sufficient that \(M'\) be so.

Indeed, we have \(M=\varinjlim_i M_{i}\), where the \(M_{i}\) are the sub-modules of finite type of \(M\). Consequently \(M'=\varinjlim_i M_{i}'\), and if \(M'\) is of finite type, \(M'\) is equal to one of the \(M_{i}'\), so that by faithful flatness \(M\) is equal to \(M_{i}\), whence \(M\) is of finite type. Consequently there exists an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
with \(L\) free of finite type, whence an exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

with \(L'\) free of finite type. If then \(M'\) is of finite presentation, \(R'\) is of finite type, so that by what precedes \(R\) is of finite type, whence \(M\) is of finite presentation. Finally, to say that \(M\) is locally free and of finite type means that it is of finite presentation and flat (cf. IV in the Noetherian case; the general case is left to the reader). Since each of these properties descends properly, so does their conjunction, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 12

The conjunction of 1 and of 10 shows that the statement 1 still remains valid when one replaces the fibred category \(\mathscr{F}\) by the fibred subcategory formed by the Modules that are quasi-coherent of finite type, resp. of finite presentation, resp. locally free of finite type, resp. locally free of given rank \(n\).

footnotes.html: We shall here take for granted the general theory of descent expounded in detail in the article of J. Giraud cited in note (5) at the foot of the page of the Foreword, a work which we shall cite as [D] in what follows. Cf. also [2] for a succinct account.

pages.title: Descent of affine preschemes over another
pages.html:
2 Descent of affine preschemes over another

Since the inverse image functor for Modules is compatible with the tensor product and other tensorial operations, theorem 1 implies various variants, obtained by considering, instead of a single quasi-coherent Module, a quasi-coherent Module or a system of quasi-coherent Modules equipped with various supplementary structures expressible by means of tensorial operations. For example, the datum of three quasi-coherent Modules \(F\), \(G\), \(H\) over \(S\) and of a pairing

\[ F\otimes G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H, \]

is equivalent to the datum of three quasi-coherent Modules \(F'\), \(G'\), \(H'\) over \(S'\), equipped with descent data relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and equipped with a pairing

\[ F'\otimes G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H', \]

“compatible” with these descent data, in the obvious sense of the term. For example, if \(F=G=H\), one sees that the datum of a quasi-coherent Module \(F\) over \(S\) equipped with an algebra law (which for the moment we do not assume to satisfy any axiom of associativity, commutativity, or of existence of a unit section), is equivalent to the same datum over \(S'\), equipped in addition with a descent datum. And using the results of the preceding section, one verifies at once that in order for \(F\) to satisfy one of the usual axioms to which we have just alluded, it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \(F'\). For example, the datum of a quasi-coherent Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) over \(S\) (by which we henceforth understand: associative, commutative, with unit section) is equivalent to the datum of a quasi-coherent Algebra \(\mathscr{A}'\) over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). If one recalls the equivalence between the dual category of quasi-coherent Algebras over \(S\), and the category of affine \(S\)-preschemes over \(S\), (EGA II par.1), one finds at once:

Theorem 1

Let \(\mathscr{F}'\) be the fibred category of affine morphisms of preschemes \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), regarded as a fibred subcategory of the fibred category of arrows in the category of preschemes \(\mathbf{Sch}\) (VI 11.a). Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes which is faithfully flat and quasi-compact. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective \(\mathscr{F}'\)-descent.

pages.title: Descent of set-theoretic properties and of finiteness properties of morphisms
pages.html:
3 Descent of set-theoretic properties and of finiteness properties of morphisms

For other results like those treated in sections 3 and 4, Cf. EGA IV 2.3, 2.6, 2.7.

Proposition 1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a surjective morphism, \(f'\colon X'=X\times_{S}S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'=Y\times_{S}S'\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the base change by means of \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). For \(f\) to be surjective (resp. radicial), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

One notes that \(f'\) can also be obtained by the base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), which is likewise surjective since it is deduced from \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) which is so. On the other hand, for every \(y\in Y\) and every \(y'\in Y'\) above \(y\), we have an isomorphism

\[ X'_{y'}\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X_{y}\otimes_{\mathit{k}(y)}\mathit{k}(y'), \]

where \(X_{y}\) denotes the fibre of \(X\) at \(y\), and \(X'_{y'}\) that of \(X'\) at \(y'\). It follows that \(X_{y}\) is non-empty (resp. has at most one point, and this last corresponds to a radicial residual extension) if and only if \(X'_{y'}\) has the same property. This proves 1.

Corollary 2

Under the conditions of 1, if \(f'\) is injective (resp. bijective), then \(f\) is so as well.

This comes from the fact that if \(X'_{y'}\) has at most one point (resp. exactly one point) the same holds for \(X_{y}\); this is indeed so, since the morphism \(X'_{y'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{y}\) is surjective (for it is deduced from \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y'))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y))\) which is so).

Proposition 3

With the notations of 1. Suppose that \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is surjective and quasi-compact (resp. faithfully flat and quasi-compact). For \(f\) to be quasi-compact (resp. of finite type), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

There is only the “sufficiency” to prove. One may evidently suppose \(S=Y\), since the hypothesis made on \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is preserved for \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). Moreover one may suppose \(Y\) affine. Then \(Y'\) is quasi-compact, hence \(X'\) is quasi-compact (since \(f'\) is so by hypothesis). Let \((X_{i})_{i\in I}\) be a family of affine open sets of \(X\) covering \(X\); then the \(X_{i}'\) are open sets of \(X'\) covering \(X'\), hence there is a finite subfamily which covers \(X'\). Since \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is surjective, it follows that the corresponding \(X_{i}\) already cover \(X\), hence \(X\) is quasi-compact, i.e. \(f\) is quasi-compact. Suppose now \(f'\) of finite type, let us prove that \(f\) is so, supposing \(g\) faithfully flat. Replacing \(Y'\) by the sum of a family of affine open sets covering it, one may suppose \(Y'\) affine. Finally, \(X\) being covered by a finite number of affine open sets \(X_{i}\) by what precedes, one must show that they are of finite type over \(Y\), knowing that \(X_{i}'\) is of finite type over \(Y'\). This then brings us back to

Corollary 4

Let \(B\) be an \(A\)-algebra, \(A'\) a faithfully flat \(A\)-algebra, \(B'=B\otimes_{A}A'\) the \(A\)-algebra deduced from \(B\) by change of ring. For \(B\) to be of finite type, it is necessary and sufficient that \(B'\) be so.

There is only the “sufficiency” to prove. We have \(B=\varinjlim_i B_{i}\), where the \(B_{i}\) are the finite-type subalgebras of \(B\). We thus have \(B'=\varinjlim_i B_{i}'\), and if \(B'\) is of finite type over \(A'\), then \(B'\) is equal to one of the \(B_{i}'\), hence \(B\) is equal to \(B_{i}\), and hence is of finite type.

Corollary 5

Suppose again the base change morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact. For \(f\) to be quasi-finite, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

Indeed, the property “quasi-finite” is by definition the conjunction of “of finite type” and “with finite fibres”, each of which descends well by \(g\), the first by virtue of 3, the second by the reasoning of 1 (using only the fact that \(g\) is surjective).

Remarks 6

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(X\) an \(A\)-prescheme. One sees easily that the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists a Noetherian ring \(A_{0}\) (which one may if one wishes suppose to be a finite-type subring of \(A\)), a finite-type \(A_{0}\)-prescheme \(X_{0}\), a homomorphism \(A_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) and an \(A\)-isomorphism \(X\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_{0}\times_{A_{0}}A\).

  2. (ii)

    The diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}X\) is quasi-compact (an empty condition if \(X\) is separated over \(A\)), \(X\) is a finite union of affine open sets \(X_{i}\) whose rings \(B_{i}\) are algebras of finite presentation over \(A\), i.e. quotients of polynomial algebras in a finite number of indeterminates, by finite-type ideals.

If \(X\) is itself affine, with ring \(B\), these conditions mean simply that \(B\) is an algebra of finite presentation over \(A\).

A morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is called a morphism of finite presentation, and one also says that \(X\) is of finite presentation over \(Y\), if \(Y\) is a union of affine open sets \(Y_{i}\), such that \(X|Y_{i}\) as a \(Y_{i}\)-prescheme satisfies the preceding equivalent conditions. It then holds likewise for \(X|Y'\) for every affine open set \(Y'\) in \(Y\). This is a property stable under base change, and moreover the composite of two morphisms of finite presentation is of finite presentation.

These notions being laid down, one sees on (ii), proceeding as in 10, that this statement remains valid upon replacing therein the words “of finite type” by “of finite presentation”.

pages.title: Descent of topological properties
pages.html:
4 Descent of topological properties

Theorem 1

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism, and \(Z\) a subset of \(Y\). Suppose that \(g\) is flat, and that there exists a quasi-compact morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that \(Z=f(X)\) (N.B. if \(Y\) is Noetherian, this last condition is implied by “ \(Z\) is constructible ”). Then we have

\[ g^{-1}(\overline{Z})=\overline{g^{-1}(Z)}. \]

We may suppose \(Y\) affine, then \(Y'\) affine. Since \(Y\) is affine, \(X\) is a finite union of affine opens \(X_{i}\), and, replacing \(X\) by the sum of the \(X_{i}\), we may likewise suppose \(X\) affine. Let \(A,A',B\) be the rings of \(Y,Y',X\), \(B'=B\otimes_{A}A'\) that of \(X'=X\times_{Y}Y'\), \(I\) the kernel of \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), \(I'\) the kernel of \(A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B'\), so that the closed subsets of \(Y\) and \(Y'\) defined by these ideals are respectively the closure of \(Z=f(X)\) and the closure of \(Z'=f'(X')=g^{-1}(Z)\). We wish to establish that the latter is equal to \(g^{-1}(\overline{Z})\), which will follow from \(I'=IA'\), itself a consequence of the flatness of \(A'\) over \(A\).

Corollary 2

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat and quasi-compact morphism, and \(Z'\) a closed subset of \(Y'\) saturated for the set-theoretic equivalence relation defined by \(g\). Then we have \(Z'=g^{-1}(\overline{g(Z')})\).

.5cmIndeed we have \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\), with \(Z=g(Z')\). We may then apply 1, noting that the condition imposed on \(Z\) in 1 is indeed satisfied by taking for \(X\) the prescheme \(Z'\) endowed with the reduced structure induced by \(Y'\). (The fact that \(g\) is quasi-compact then ensures that the induced morphism \(f\colon Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is quasi-compact).

Statement 2 also means that the topology of \(g(Y')\) induced by \(Y\) is a quotient of that of \(Y'\). In particular:

Corollary 3

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Then \(g\) makes \(Y\) a topological quotient space of \(Y'\), i.e. for a subset \(Z\) of \(Y\), \(Z\) is closed (resp. open) if and only if \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\) is.

Let us now recall that two elements \(a,b\), of \(Y'\) have the same image in \(Y\) if and only if they are of the form \(p_{1}(c),p_{2}(c)\) for a suitable element \(c\) in \(Y''=Y'\times_{Y}Y'\). It follows that if \(g\) is surjective, we have an exact diagram of sets

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{P}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathscr{P}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathscr{P}(Y'')}}, \]

where for every set \(E\), we denote by \(\mathscr{P}(E)\) the set of its subsets. This being posed, 3 can also be interpreted as follows:

Corollary 4 (Descent of open resp. closed subsets)

Let \(g\colon Y'{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} Y\) be as in 3. For every prescheme \(X\), let \(\operatorname{Ouv}(X)\) resp. \(\operatorname{Fer}(X)\) be the set of its open subsets resp. the set of its closed subsets. Then we have exact diagrams of set-theoretic maps (deduced from \(g\) and from the two projections of \(Y''=Y'\times_{Y}Y'\)): -5pt

\begin{gather*} \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y'')}}\\[-3pt] \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Fer}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Fer}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Fer}(Y'')}} \end{gather*}

We have the following complement to 3:

Corollary 5

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be as in 3, and let \(Z\) be a subset of \(Y\) such that there exists a quasi-compact morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) with image \(Z\) (for example \(Z\) constructible, \(Y\) Noetherian). In order for \(Z\) to be a locally closed subset of \(Y\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\) be a locally closed subset of \(Y'\).

It suffices to prove the “ sufficiency ”. Let \(Y_{1}\) be the closed sub-prescheme of \(Y\), the closure of \(Z\) endowed with the induced reduced structure, and let \(Y_{1}'=Y_{1}\times_{Y}Y'\) be the closed sub-prescheme of \(Y'\), the inverse image of \(Y_{1}\). Its underlying set is the inverse image \(g^{-1}(Y_{1})=g^{-1}(\overline{Z})\), hence is equal, by virtue of 1, to \(\overline{Z'}\). Since \(Z'\) is locally closed in \(Y'\), it is open in \(\overline{Z'}\) hence open in \(Y_{1}'\). But this last is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y_{1}\), hence by virtue of 3 we conclude that \(Z\) is open in \(Y_{1}\), i.e. in \(\overline{Z}\), which means that \(Z\) is locally closed.

Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by base change. Suppose that \(f'\) is an open map (resp. a closed map, resp. quasi-compact and a homeomorphism into, resp. a homeomorphism onto); then \(f\) has the same property.

Since \(Y'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\), we may suppose \(Y=S\). Let \(Q\) be a subset of \(X\); we then have (denoting by \(h\) the projection morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\)):

\[ g^{-1}(f(Q))=f'(h^{-1}(Q)). \]

If \(Q\) is open (resp. closed), so is \(h^{-1}(Q)\), hence so is \(f'(h^{-1}(Q))\) if we assume \(f'\) an open (resp. closed) map, hence so is \(f(Q)\) by virtue of the preceding formula and of 3. This proves the first two assertions in 6; it remains to examine the case where \(f'\) is a homeomorphism into, and to prove then that \(f\) is a homeomorphism into. (The case of a homeomorphism onto will then follow from 1). By virtue of 2 \(f\) is injective; it remains to prove that the map \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f(X)\) is open. We already know that \(f\) is quasi-compact by virtue of 3. It suffices to prove that for every closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\), we have \(Z=f^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)})\), which is equivalent to the analogous formula for the inverse images by the surjective map \(h\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), i.e. to

\[ Z'=f'^{-1}(g^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)}), \]

where we set \(Z'=h^{-1}(Z)\). Now by virtue of 1 applied to the subset \(f(Z)\) of \(Y\), we have \(g^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)})=\overline{g^{-1}(f(Z))}\), and the formula to be proved is equivalent to

\[ Z'=f'^{-1}(\overline{f'(Z')}), \]

which follows from the hypothesis that \(f'\) is a homeomorphism into.

N.B. In this last argument, already supposing that \(f\) is quasi-compact, we did not use that \(g\) is quasi-compact, but only that \(g\) is faithfully flat. So it is under this hypothesis that one may descend the property “ homeomorphism into ”, or “ homeomorphism onto ”, or again, thanks to the preceding argument, the property “ \(f'\) is quasi-compact and makes \(f'(X')\) a topological quotient space of \(X'\) ”.

We shall say that a morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of preschemes is universally open (resp. universally closed, resp. universally bicontinuous, etc.) if for every base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) is an open morphism (resp. closed, resp. a homeomorphism onto the image space). We then deduce from 6:

Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 6, in order that \(f\) be universally open, (resp. universally closed, resp. a universal homeomorphism onto, resp. a universal homeomorphism), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 6, in order that \(f\) be separated (resp. proper) it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

To say that \(f\) is separated means that the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_{Y}X\) is closed or also universally closed, and the first assertion of 8 therefore results from 7. To say that \(f\) is proper means that \(f\) satisfies the conditions a) \(f\) is of finite type b) \(f\) is separated c) \(f\) is universally closed. Condition a) descends well by virtue of 3, b) also according to what we have just seen, finally c) likewise by 7.

Remarks 9

Let us recall that when \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a flat morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, then \(g\) is an open morphism (VI 6) which is a more precise result than 3. We shall note however that if \(f\) is a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism of Noetherian preschemes, then \(f\) is not in general an open morphism. Let for example \(Y\) be an irreducible scheme whose generic point \(y\) is not open (for example an algebraic curve), and take for \(Y'\) the sum scheme \(Y\amalg \operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y))\), then the image under the structural morphism \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of the open part \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y))\) is not an open part of \(Y\). The reader will also note that various statements of the present exposé become false if one abandons in them the hypothesis that the faithfully flat morphism considered is also quasi-compact, the typical case putting the statements at fault being that where one takes for \(Y'\) the sum scheme of the spectra of the local rings of the points of \(Y\). For example, taking again for \(Y\) an irreducible algebraic curve, and for \(Z\) the part of \(Y\) reduced to the generic point, its inverse image in \(Y'\) is open, without \(Z\) being open.

10

Various statements of the present exposé remain valid on replacing in them the hypothesis that \(Y'\) be flat over \(Y\) by the following one: there exists a Module of finite type \(F\) on \(Y'\), of support \(Y'\), flat relative to \(Y\); the hypothesis of faithful flatness will then be replaced by the preceding one, plus the hypothesis that \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is surjective. This applies to the first two assertions in 10, to 3, 5, 1 and consequently to all the results of the present number.

blocks.html: 4 Descent of topological properties

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism, and \(Z\) a subset of \(Y\). Suppose that \(g\) is flat, and that there exists a quasi-compact morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that \(Z=f(X)\) (N.B. if \(Y\) is Noetherian, this last condition is implied by “ \(Z\) is constructible ”). Then we have

\[ g^{-1}(\overline{Z})=\overline{g^{-1}(Z)}. \]

We may suppose \(Y\) affine, then \(Y'\) affine. Since \(Y\) is affine, \(X\) is a finite union of affine opens \(X_{i}\), and, replacing \(X\) by the sum of the \(X_{i}\), we may likewise suppose \(X\) affine. Let \(A,A',B\) be the rings of \(Y,Y',X\), \(B'=B\otimes_{A}A'\) that of \(X'=X\times_{Y}Y'\), \(I\) the kernel of \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), \(I'\) the kernel of \(A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B'\), so that the closed subsets of \(Y\) and \(Y'\) defined by these ideals are respectively the closure of \(Z=f(X)\) and the closure of \(Z'=f'(X')=g^{-1}(Z)\). We wish to establish that the latter is equal to \(g^{-1}(\overline{Z})\), which will follow from \(I'=IA'\), itself a consequence of the flatness of \(A'\) over \(A\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a flat and quasi-compact morphism, and \(Z'\) a closed subset of \(Y'\) saturated for the set-theoretic equivalence relation defined by \(g\). Then we have \(Z'=g^{-1}(\overline{g(Z')})\).

.5cmIndeed we have \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\), with \(Z=g(Z')\). We may then apply 1, noting that the condition imposed on \(Z\) in 1 is indeed satisfied by taking for \(X\) the prescheme \(Z'\) endowed with the reduced structure induced by \(Y'\). (The fact that \(g\) is quasi-compact then ensures that the induced morphism \(f\colon Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is quasi-compact).
Statement 2 also means that the topology of \(g(Y')\) induced by \(Y\) is a quotient of that of \(Y'\). In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Then \(g\) makes \(Y\) a topological quotient space of \(Y'\), i.e. for a subset \(Z\) of \(Y\), \(Z\) is closed (resp. open) if and only if \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\) is.

Let us now recall that two elements \(a,b\), of \(Y'\) have the same image in \(Y\) if and only if they are of the form \(p_{1}(c),p_{2}(c)\) for a suitable element \(c\) in \(Y''=Y'\times_{Y}Y'\). It follows that if \(g\) is surjective, we have an exact diagram of sets
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{P}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathscr{P}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathscr{P}(Y'')}}, \]
where for every set \(E\), we denote by \(\mathscr{P}(E)\) the set of its subsets. This being posed, 3 can also be interpreted as follows:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4 (Descent of open resp. closed subsets)

Let \(g\colon Y'{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} Y\) be as in 3. For every prescheme \(X\), let \(\operatorname{Ouv}(X)\) resp. \(\operatorname{Fer}(X)\) be the set of its open subsets resp. the set of its closed subsets. Then we have exact diagrams of set-theoretic maps (deduced from \(g\) and from the two projections of \(Y''=Y'\times_{Y}Y'\)): -5pt

\begin{gather*} \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(Y'')}}\\[-3pt] \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Fer}(Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Fer}(Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Fer}(Y'')}} \end{gather*}
We have the following complement to 3:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be as in 3, and let \(Z\) be a subset of \(Y\) such that there exists a quasi-compact morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) with image \(Z\) (for example \(Z\) constructible, \(Y\) Noetherian). In order for \(Z\) to be a locally closed subset of \(Y\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(Z'=g^{-1}(Z)\) be a locally closed subset of \(Y'\).

It suffices to prove the “ sufficiency ”. Let \(Y_{1}\) be the closed sub-prescheme of \(Y\), the closure of \(Z\) endowed with the induced reduced structure, and let \(Y_{1}'=Y_{1}\times_{Y}Y'\) be the closed sub-prescheme of \(Y'\), the inverse image of \(Y_{1}\). Its underlying set is the inverse image \(g^{-1}(Y_{1})=g^{-1}(\overline{Z})\), hence is equal, by virtue of 1, to \(\overline{Z'}\). Since \(Z'\) is locally closed in \(Y'\), it is open in \(\overline{Z'}\) hence open in \(Y_{1}'\). But this last is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y_{1}\), hence by virtue of 3 we conclude that \(Z\) is open in \(Y_{1}\), i.e. in \(\overline{Z}\), which means that \(Z\) is locally closed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by base change. Suppose that \(f'\) is an open map (resp. a closed map, resp. quasi-compact and a homeomorphism into, resp. a homeomorphism onto); then \(f\) has the same property.

Since \(Y'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\), we may suppose \(Y=S\). Let \(Q\) be a subset of \(X\); we then have (denoting by \(h\) the projection morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\)):
\[ g^{-1}(f(Q))=f'(h^{-1}(Q)). \]

If \(Q\) is open (resp. closed), so is \(h^{-1}(Q)\), hence so is \(f'(h^{-1}(Q))\) if we assume \(f'\) an open (resp. closed) map, hence so is \(f(Q)\) by virtue of the preceding formula and of 3. This proves the first two assertions in 6; it remains to examine the case where \(f'\) is a homeomorphism into, and to prove then that \(f\) is a homeomorphism into. (The case of a homeomorphism onto will then follow from 1). By virtue of 2 \(f\) is injective; it remains to prove that the map \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f(X)\) is open. We already know that \(f\) is quasi-compact by virtue of 3. It suffices to prove that for every closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\), we have \(Z=f^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)})\), which is equivalent to the analogous formula for the inverse images by the surjective map \(h\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), i.e. to
\[ Z'=f'^{-1}(g^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)}), \]
where we set \(Z'=h^{-1}(Z)\). Now by virtue of 1 applied to the subset \(f(Z)\) of \(Y\), we have \(g^{-1}(\overline{f(Z)})=\overline{g^{-1}(f(Z))}\), and the formula to be proved is equivalent to
\[ Z'=f'^{-1}(\overline{f'(Z')}), \]
which follows from the hypothesis that \(f'\) is a homeomorphism into.

N.B. In this last argument, already supposing that \(f\) is quasi-compact, we did not use that \(g\) is quasi-compact, but only that \(g\) is faithfully flat. So it is under this hypothesis that one may descend the property “ homeomorphism into ”, or “ homeomorphism onto ”, or again, thanks to the preceding argument, the property “ \(f'\) is quasi-compact and makes \(f'(X')\) a topological quotient space of \(X'\) ”.

We shall say that a morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of preschemes is universally open (resp. universally closed, resp. universally bicontinuous, etc.) if for every base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) is an open morphism (resp. closed, resp. a homeomorphism onto the image space). We then deduce from 6:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 6, in order that \(f\) be universally open, (resp. universally closed, resp. a universal homeomorphism onto, resp. a universal homeomorphism), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 6, in order that \(f\) be separated (resp. proper) it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

To say that \(f\) is separated means that the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_{Y}X\) is closed or also universally closed, and the first assertion of 8 therefore results from 7. To say that \(f\) is proper means that \(f\) satisfies the conditions a) \(f\) is of finite type b) \(f\) is separated c) \(f\) is universally closed. Condition a) descends well by virtue of 3, b) also according to what we have just seen, finally c) likewise by 7.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 9

Let us recall that when \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a flat morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, then \(g\) is an open morphism (VI 6) which is a more precise result than 3. We shall note however that if \(f\) is a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism of Noetherian preschemes, then \(f\) is not in general an open morphism. Let for example \(Y\) be an irreducible scheme whose generic point \(y\) is not open (for example an algebraic curve), and take for \(Y'\) the sum scheme \(Y\amalg \operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y))\), then the image under the structural morphism \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of the open part \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(y))\) is not an open part of \(Y\). The reader will also note that various statements of the present exposé become false if one abandons in them the hypothesis that the faithfully flat morphism considered is also quasi-compact, the typical case putting the statements at fault being that where one takes for \(Y'\) the sum scheme of the spectra of the local rings of the points of \(Y\). For example, taking again for \(Y\) an irreducible algebraic curve, and for \(Z\) the part of \(Y\) reduced to the generic point, its inverse image in \(Y'\) is open, without \(Z\) being open.

Various statements of the present exposé remain valid on replacing in them the hypothesis that \(Y'\) be flat over \(Y\) by the following one: there exists a Module of finite type \(F\) on \(Y'\), of support \(Y'\), flat relative to \(Y\); the hypothesis of faithful flatness will then be replaced by the preceding one, plus the hypothesis that \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is surjective. This applies to the first two assertions in 10, to 3, 5, 1 and consequently to all the results of the present number.

pages.title: Descent of morphisms of preschemes
pages.html:
5 Descent of morphisms of preschemes

Proposition 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes.

  1. a)

    Suppose that \(g\) is surjective, and that the homomorphism

    \[ g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \]

    is injective; then \(g\) is an epimorphism in the category of preschemes, and even in the category of ringed spaces.

  2. b)

    Suppose that \(g\) is surjective and makes \(S\) a topological quotient space of \(S'\). Let \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\) and let \(h\colon S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the structural morphism; consider the canonical diagram of homomorphisms:

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{O}_{S}}\ar[r]&{g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(\mathscr{O}_{S''})}}, \]

    and suppose this diagram exact. Then \(g\) is an effective epimorphism in the category of preschemes (and also in the category of ringed spaces), i.e. the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S}&{S'}\ar[l]&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]} \]

    is exact.

Proof

a) We must show that a morphism of ringed spaces \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is known when one knows \(fg\). Now since \(g\) is surjective, one knows the underlying set-theoretic map \(f_{0}\) of \(f\); it remains to determine the homomorphism of sheaves of rings \(\mathscr{O}_{Z}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S}\), or what amounts to the same thing the homomorphism of sheaves of rings

\[ u\colon f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S} \]

defined by \(f\). One already knows the homomorphism

\[ (fg)_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})= g_{0}^{-1}(f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S'} \]

defined by \(fg\), or what amounts to the same thing, one has a homomorphism

\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_{0*}(\mathscr{O}_{S'})=g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}). \]

One notes at once that this last one is none other than the composite of \(g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})\) and of \(u\), and since \(g^*\) is injective, \(u\) is known when one knows \(g^*u\). [N.B. we have of course not used that \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of preschemes, the statement would hold for an arbitrary morphism of ringed spaces; the same remark holds for b), both in the category of ringed spaces and in the category of ringed spaces with local rings. Note also that if \(g\) is a morphism of preschemes not necessarily surjective, but such that \(g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})\) is injective, then for two morphisms \(f_{1},f_{2}\) of \(S\) into a scheme \(Z\) such that \(f_{1}g=f_{2}g\), one has \(f_{1}=f_{2}\); indeed, if \(I\) is the Ideal on \(S\) which defines the sub-prescheme of \(S\) of the coincidences of \(f_{1},f_{2}\) (inverse image of the diagonal sub-prescheme of \(Z\times Z\) under \((f_{1},f_{2})\)), one sees that \(I\) is contained in \(\operatorname{Ker}(g^*)\)].

b) We must show that for every ringed space \(Z\), the following diagram of maps

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}(S,Z)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}(S',Z)}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}(S'',Z)}} \]

is exact, and that the same holds when \(Z\) is a ringed space with local rings and one restricts to homomorphisms of ringed spaces with local rings. Since we already know by a) that the first map is injective, it remains to see that if \(f'\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a homomorphism of ringed spaces such that \(f'p_{1}=f'p_{2}\), then \(f'\) is of the form \(fg\), where \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a homomorphism of ringed spaces. Since \(g\) is surjective, it is then evident that if \(f'\) is a morphism of ringed spaces with local rings, the same will hold for \(f\).

From the hypothesis on \(f'\) it follows that the underlying set-theoretic map \(f_{0}'\) is constant on the fibres of the map \(g_{0}\), hence since the latter is surjective, \(f_{0}'\) factors uniquely as \(f_{0}'=f_{0}g_{0}\), where \(f_{0}\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a map, necessarily continuous since \(g_{0}\) identifies \(S\) with a topological quotient space of \(S'\). Let us now consider the homomorphism

\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \]

deduced from the homomorphism \((f_{0}g_{0})^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S'}\) corresponding to \(f'\). The hypothesis \(f'p_{1}=f'p_{2}\) is then interpreted by saying that the composites of the preceding homomorphism with the two homomorphisms \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(\mathscr{O}_{S''})}}\) are the same, hence by hypothesis b) it factors through a morphism

\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S}. \]

This last one defines a morphism of ringed spaces \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), which is the sought morphism.

Theorem 2

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be the fibred category of arrows in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}\) of preschemes (VI 11.a). Then every faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent (or again, as one says, a morphism of descent in \(\mathbf{Sch}\)).

This therefore means the following: let \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\), and for two preschemes \(X,Y\) over \(S\), consider their inverse images \(X',Y'\) over \(S'\) and their inverse images \(X'',Y''\) over \(S''\), whence a diagram of maps

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_{S}(X,Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(X'',Y'')}}; \]

with these notations laid down, 2 means that this diagram is exact. One will note that it is not true in general that \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent, i.e. that for every prescheme \(X'\) over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is effective. The question of effectivity, often delicate, will be examined in 7.

It has been seen [D], (taking into account that in \(\mathbf{Sch}\) the fibred products exist) that statement 2 is equivalent to the following:

Corollary 3

A faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism of preschemes is a universal effective epi­mor­phism.

Since a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism remains such under every extension of the base, we are reduced to proving that it is an effective epimorphism. One then applies criterion 1 b), which gives the desired result, taking into account 3 and 7.

Corollary 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by the base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). For \(f\) to be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be one.

Indeed, if \(f'\) is an isomorphism, it is also an isomorphism for the natural descent structures on \(X',Y'\), and since the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\) from \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) into the category of preschemes over \(S'\) equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\) is fully faithful by 2, the desired conclusion appears.

Corollary 5

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be a closed immersion (resp. an open immersion, resp. a quasi-compact immersion), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be one.

As usual one may suppose \(Y=S\), and it remains only to prove the “sufficiency”. Let us note that the fact that \(X'/Y'\) is equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), and that the structural morphism \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) be an immersion, hence a monomorphism, implies that the two subobjects of \(Y''\) inverse images of \(X'/Y'\) under the one and the other projection of \(S''\) into \(S'\) are the same. If \(f'\) is a closed immersion, it follows by virtue of 9 that there exists a closed sub-prescheme \(X_{1}\) of \(Y\) whose inverse image under \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is \(X'\). Hence, by the uniqueness of the solution of a descent problem relative to a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, it follows that \(X_{1}\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to \(X\), hence \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a closed immersion. One proceeds in the same way for an open immersion, using 4. If finally \(f'\) is a quasi-compact immersion, \(f\) is quasi-compact by virtue of 3, so one may apply to the part \(f(X)\) of \(Y\) the criterion 5, which proves that \(f(X)\) is locally closed since its inverse image \(f'(X')\) in \(Y'\) is. Replacing then \(Y\) by an open part in which \(f(X)\) is closed, one is reduced to the case where \(f'\) is a closed immersion, hence \(f\) is one by virtue of what precedes.

Corollary 6

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be affine, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

One proceeds as in 5, using 1 (One may also use Serre's cohomological criterion [EGA II 5.2], which proves 6 without using any descent technique).

Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be integral (resp. finite, resp. finite and locally free), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

It remains only to prove the “sufficiency”, and as usual one may suppose \(Y=S\), \(Y\) affine, and \(Y'\) affine. Since the hypothesis implies that \(f'\) is affine, the same holds for \(f\) by 6, hence \(X\) and consequently \(X'\) are affine. Let \(A,A',B,B'=B\otimes_{A}A'\) be the rings of \(Y,Y',X,X'\). We have \(B=\varinjlim_i B_{i}\), where \(B_{i}\) ranges over the sub-\(A\)-algebras of \(B\) that are of finite type over \(A\), whence \(B'=\varinjlim_i B_{i}'\), where the \(B_{i}'\) are sub-algebras of finite type of the \(A'\)-algebra \(B'\). If \(B'\) is integral over \(A'\), the \(B_{i}'\) are modules of finite type over \(A'\), hence, \(A'\) being faithfully flat over \(A\), the \(B_{i}\) are modules of finite type over \(A\), i.e. \(B\) is integral over \(A\). One sees in the same way that if \(B'\) is finite over \(A'\), \(B\) is so over \(A\). The same conclusion for “locally free of finite type”, cf. 11.

Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 4, suppose \(f\) quasi-compact and let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an invertible Module on \(X\), and \(\mathscr{L}'\) its inverse image on \(X'\). In order that \(\mathscr{L}\) be ample (resp. very ample) relative to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{L}'\) be ample (resp. very ample) relative to \(f'\).

It remains only to prove the “sufficiency”. The hypothesis on \(\mathscr{L}\) implies in any case that \(f'\) is separated, hence \(f\) is separated by 8, and since \(f\) is quasi-compact, and \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is flat, the computation of the direct images by affine coverings shows that one has isomorphisms

\[ g^*(f_*(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes n}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} f_*'(\mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n}) \]

for every integer \(n\), hence one has an isomorphism

\[ g^*(\mathscr{S})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{S}', \]

where \(\mathscr{S}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{S}'\)) denotes the quasi-coherent graded Algebra on \(Y\) (resp. on \(Y'\)) the direct sum of the \(f_*(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes n})\) (resp. of the \(f_*'(\mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n})\)) for \(n\geqslant 0\). Let us note that for every \(n\geqslant 0\), the cokernel of the canonical homomorphism \(f'^*(\mathscr{S}_{n}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n}\) is the inverse image under \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of the cokernel of \(f^*(\mathscr{S}_{n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{L}^{\otimes n}\), hence its support \(Z_{n}'\) is the inverse image of the support \(Z_{n}\). If \(\mathscr{L}'\) is ample the intersection of the \(Z_{n}'\) is empty, hence since \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is surjective, the intersection of the \(Z_{n}\) is empty, i.e. one has a canonical morphism

\[ j\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}) \]

(EGA II 3). Moreover, the analogous morphism

\[ j'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}') \]

is none other than the one deduced from the preceding one by the base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) (loc. cit.). This being posited, to say that \(\mathscr{L}'\) is ample relative to \(f'\) means that \(j'\) is an immersion, moreover necessarily quasi-compact since \(f'\) is quasi-compact. Hence by virtue of 5 \(j\) is an immersion, i.e. \(\mathscr{L}\) is ample relative to \(f\). — One proceeds in an entirely analogous way in the case of “very ample”, restricting oneself above to \(n=1\), and replacing the consideration of \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\) by that of the projective bundle \(\mathscr{P}(\mathscr{S}_{1})\) associated with \(\mathscr{S}_{1}\).

Let us recall (EGA II 5.1.1) that a quasi-compact morphism \(f\) is said to be quasi-affine if for every affine open \(U\) in \(Y\), \(f^{-1}(U)\) is a prescheme isomorphic to an open subscheme of an affine scheme. One shows (loc. cit.) that it amounts to the same to say that \(\mathscr{O}_{X}\) is ample (or also: very ample) relative to \(f\). Hence 8 implies:

Corollary 9

Under the conditions of 4, and supposing \(f\) quasi-compact, in order that \(f\) be quasi-affine, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

Remarks 10

Hironaka's example of a non-projective variety shows that one can have a proper morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of non-singular algebraic varieties (with \(Y\) projective), such that \(Y\) is the union of two opens \(Y_{i}\) such that \(X_{i}=X\times_{Y}Y_{i}\) is projective over \(Y_{i}\), but with \(f\) not being projective. Hence, setting \(Y'=Y_{1}\amalg Y_{2}\), \(Y'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact (and even quasi-finite) over \(Y\), \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) is projective, but \(f\) is not projective. One must therefore be careful that in order to apply 8, and to deduce from the fact that \(f'\) is projective the same conclusion on \(f\), one must already have available on \(X'\) an invertible Module \(\mathscr{L}'\) ample for \(f'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), (which permits one to consider \(\mathscr{L}'\) as the inverse image of an invertible Module \(\mathscr{L}\) on \(X\), which will then be ample for \(f\) thanks to 8). When \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite and locally free, see nevertheless 7.

blocks.html: 5 Descent of morphisms of preschemes

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes.

  1. a)

    Suppose that \(g\) is surjective, and that the homomorphism

    \[ g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \]

    is injective; then \(g\) is an epimorphism in the category of preschemes, and even in the category of ringed spaces.

  2. b)

    Suppose that \(g\) is surjective and makes \(S\) a topological quotient space of \(S'\). Let \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\) and let \(h\colon S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the structural morphism; consider the canonical diagram of homomorphisms:

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{O}_{S}}\ar[r]&{g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(\mathscr{O}_{S''})}}, \]

    and suppose this diagram exact. Then \(g\) is an effective epimorphism in the category of preschemes (and also in the category of ringed spaces), i.e. the diagram

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S}&{S'}\ar[l]&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]} \]

    is exact.
Proof

a) We must show that a morphism of ringed spaces \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is known when one knows \(fg\). Now since \(g\) is surjective, one knows the underlying set-theoretic map \(f_{0}\) of \(f\); it remains to determine the homomorphism of sheaves of rings \(\mathscr{O}_{Z}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S}\), or what amounts to the same thing the homomorphism of sheaves of rings
\[ u\colon f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S} \]
defined by \(f\). One already knows the homomorphism
\[ (fg)_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})= g_{0}^{-1}(f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S'} \]
defined by \(fg\), or what amounts to the same thing, one has a homomorphism
\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_{0*}(\mathscr{O}_{S'})=g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}). \]

One notes at once that this last one is none other than the composite of \(g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})\) and of \(u\), and since \(g^*\) is injective, \(u\) is known when one knows \(g^*u\). [N.B. we have of course not used that \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of preschemes, the statement would hold for an arbitrary morphism of ringed spaces; the same remark holds for b), both in the category of ringed spaces and in the category of ringed spaces with local rings. Note also that if \(g\) is a morphism of preschemes not necessarily surjective, but such that \(g^*\colon \mathscr{O}_{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})\) is injective, then for two morphisms \(f_{1},f_{2}\) of \(S\) into a scheme \(Z\) such that \(f_{1}g=f_{2}g\), one has \(f_{1}=f_{2}\); indeed, if \(I\) is the Ideal on \(S\) which defines the sub-prescheme of \(S\) of the coincidences of \(f_{1},f_{2}\) (inverse image of the diagonal sub-prescheme of \(Z\times Z\) under \((f_{1},f_{2})\)), one sees that \(I\) is contained in \(\operatorname{Ker}(g^*)\)].
b) We must show that for every ringed space \(Z\), the following diagram of maps
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}(S,Z)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}(S',Z)}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}(S'',Z)}} \]

is exact, and that the same holds when \(Z\) is a ringed space with local rings and one restricts to homomorphisms of ringed spaces with local rings. Since we already know by a) that the first map is injective, it remains to see that if \(f'\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a homomorphism of ringed spaces such that \(f'p_{1}=f'p_{2}\), then \(f'\) is of the form \(fg\), where \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a homomorphism of ringed spaces. Since \(g\) is surjective, it is then evident that if \(f'\) is a morphism of ringed spaces with local rings, the same will hold for \(f\).

From the hypothesis on \(f'\) it follows that the underlying set-theoretic map \(f_{0}'\) is constant on the fibres of the map \(g_{0}\), hence since the latter is surjective, \(f_{0}'\) factors uniquely as \(f_{0}'=f_{0}g_{0}\), where \(f_{0}\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is a map, necessarily continuous since \(g_{0}\) identifies \(S\) with a topological quotient space of \(S'\). Let us now consider the homomorphism
\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'}) \]

deduced from the homomorphism \((f_{0}g_{0})^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S'}\) corresponding to \(f'\). The hypothesis \(f'p_{1}=f'p_{2}\) is then interpreted by saying that the composites of the preceding homomorphism with the two homomorphisms \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{g_*(\mathscr{O}_{S'})}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{h_*(\mathscr{O}_{S''})}}\) are the same, hence by hypothesis b) it factors through a morphism
\[ f_{0}^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{S}. \]
This last one defines a morphism of ringed spaces \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), which is the sought morphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2
