Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be the fibred category of arrows in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}\) of preschemes (VI 11.a). Then every faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent (or again, as one says, a morphism of descent in \(\mathbf{Sch}\)).

This therefore means the following: let \(S''=S'\times_{S}S'\), and for two preschemes \(X,Y\) over \(S\), consider their inverse images \(X',Y'\) over \(S'\) and their inverse images \(X'',Y''\) over \(S''\), whence a diagram of maps
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_{S}(X,Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(X'',Y'')}}; \]

with these notations laid down, 2 means that this diagram is exact. One will note that it is not true in general that \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent, i.e. that for every prescheme \(X'\) over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is effective. The question of effectivity, often delicate, will be examined in 7.
It has been seen [D], (taking into account that in \(\mathbf{Sch}\) the fibred products exist) that statement 2 is equivalent to the following:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

A faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism of preschemes is a universal effective epi­mor­phism.

Since a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism remains such under every extension of the base, we are reduced to proving that it is an effective epimorphism. One then applies criterion 1 b), which gives the desired result, taking into account 3 and 7.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by the base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). For \(f\) to be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be one.

Indeed, if \(f'\) is an isomorphism, it is also an isomorphism for the natural descent structures on \(X',Y'\), and since the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\) from \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) into the category of preschemes over \(S'\) equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\) is fully faithful by 2, the desired conclusion appears.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be a closed immersion (resp. an open immersion, resp. a quasi-compact immersion), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be one.

As usual one may suppose \(Y=S\), and it remains only to prove the “sufficiency”. Let us note that the fact that \(X'/Y'\) is equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), and that the structural morphism \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) be an immersion, hence a monomorphism, implies that the two subobjects of \(Y''\) inverse images of \(X'/Y'\) under the one and the other projection of \(S''\) into \(S'\) are the same. If \(f'\) is a closed immersion, it follows by virtue of 9 that there exists a closed sub-prescheme \(X_{1}\) of \(Y\) whose inverse image under \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is \(X'\). Hence, by the uniqueness of the solution of a descent problem relative to a morphism of \(\mathscr{F}\)-descent, it follows that \(X_{1}\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to \(X\), hence \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a closed immersion. One proceeds in the same way for an open immersion, using 4. If finally \(f'\) is a quasi-compact immersion, \(f\) is quasi-compact by virtue of 3, so one may apply to the part \(f(X)\) of \(Y\) the criterion 5, which proves that \(f(X)\) is locally closed since its inverse image \(f'(X')\) in \(Y'\) is. Replacing then \(Y\) by an open part in which \(f(X)\) is closed, one is reduced to the case where \(f'\) is a closed immersion, hence \(f\) is one by virtue of what precedes.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be affine, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.
One proceeds as in 5, using 1 (One may also use Serre's cohomological criterion [EGA II 5.2], which proves 6 without using any descent technique).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 4, in order that \(f\) be integral (resp. finite, resp. finite and locally free), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

It remains only to prove the “sufficiency”, and as usual one may suppose \(Y=S\), \(Y\) affine, and \(Y'\) affine. Since the hypothesis implies that \(f'\) is affine, the same holds for \(f\) by 6, hence \(X\) and consequently \(X'\) are affine. Let \(A,A',B,B'=B\otimes_{A}A'\) be the rings of \(Y,Y',X,X'\). We have \(B=\varinjlim_i B_{i}\), where \(B_{i}\) ranges over the sub-\(A\)-algebras of \(B\) that are of finite type over \(A\), whence \(B'=\varinjlim_i B_{i}'\), where the \(B_{i}'\) are sub-algebras of finite type of the \(A'\)-algebra \(B'\). If \(B'\) is integral over \(A'\), the \(B_{i}'\) are modules of finite type over \(A'\), hence, \(A'\) being faithfully flat over \(A\), the \(B_{i}\) are modules of finite type over \(A\), i.e. \(B\) is integral over \(A\). One sees in the same way that if \(B'\) is finite over \(A'\), \(B\) is so over \(A\). The same conclusion for “locally free of finite type”, cf. 11.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Under the conditions of 4, suppose \(f\) quasi-compact and let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an invertible Module on \(X\), and \(\mathscr{L}'\) its inverse image on \(X'\). In order that \(\mathscr{L}\) be ample (resp. very ample) relative to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{L}'\) be ample (resp. very ample) relative to \(f'\).

It remains only to prove the “sufficiency”. The hypothesis on \(\mathscr{L}\) implies in any case that \(f'\) is separated, hence \(f\) is separated by 8, and since \(f\) is quasi-compact, and \(g\colon Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is flat, the computation of the direct images by affine coverings shows that one has isomorphisms
\[ g^*(f_*(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes n}))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} f_*'(\mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n}) \]
for every integer \(n\), hence one has an isomorphism
\[ g^*(\mathscr{S})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{S}', \]

where \(\mathscr{S}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{S}'\)) denotes the quasi-coherent graded Algebra on \(Y\) (resp. on \(Y'\)) the direct sum of the \(f_*(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes n})\) (resp. of the \(f_*'(\mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n})\)) for \(n\geqslant 0\). Let us note that for every \(n\geqslant 0\), the cokernel of the canonical homomorphism \(f'^*(\mathscr{S}_{n}')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{L}'^{\otimes n}\) is the inverse image under \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of the cokernel of \(f^*(\mathscr{S}_{n})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{L}^{\otimes n}\), hence its support \(Z_{n}'\) is the inverse image of the support \(Z_{n}\). If \(\mathscr{L}'\) is ample the intersection of the \(Z_{n}'\) is empty, hence since \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is surjective, the intersection of the \(Z_{n}\) is empty, i.e. one has a canonical morphism
\[ j\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}) \]
(EGA II 3). Moreover, the analogous morphism
\[ j'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}') \]

is none other than the one deduced from the preceding one by the base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) (loc. cit.). This being posited, to say that \(\mathscr{L}'\) is ample relative to \(f'\) means that \(j'\) is an immersion, moreover necessarily quasi-compact since \(f'\) is quasi-compact. Hence by virtue of 5 \(j\) is an immersion, i.e. \(\mathscr{L}\) is ample relative to \(f\). — One proceeds in an entirely analogous way in the case of “very ample”, restricting oneself above to \(n=1\), and replacing the consideration of \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\) by that of the projective bundle \(\mathscr{P}(\mathscr{S}_{1})\) associated with \(\mathscr{S}_{1}\).

Let us recall (EGA II 5.1.1) that a quasi-compact morphism \(f\) is said to be quasi-affine if for every affine open \(U\) in \(Y\), \(f^{-1}(U)\) is a prescheme isomorphic to an open subscheme of an affine scheme. One shows (loc. cit.) that it amounts to the same to say that \(\mathscr{O}_{X}\) is ample (or also: very ample) relative to \(f\). Hence 8 implies:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Under the conditions of 4, and supposing \(f\) quasi-compact, in order that \(f\) be quasi-affine, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 10

Hironaka's example of a non-projective variety shows that one can have a proper morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of non-singular algebraic varieties (with \(Y\) projective), such that \(Y\) is the union of two opens \(Y_{i}\) such that \(X_{i}=X\times_{Y}Y_{i}\) is projective over \(Y_{i}\), but with \(f\) not being projective. Hence, setting \(Y'=Y_{1}\amalg Y_{2}\), \(Y'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact (and even quasi-finite) over \(Y\), \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) is projective, but \(f\) is not projective. One must therefore be careful that in order to apply 8, and to deduce from the fact that \(f'\) is projective the same conclusion on \(f\), one must already have available on \(X'\) an invertible Module \(\mathscr{L}'\) ample for \(f'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), (which permits one to consider \(\mathscr{L}'\) as the inverse image of an invertible Module \(\mathscr{L}\) on \(X\), which will then be ample for \(f\) thanks to 8). When \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite and locally free, see nevertheless 7.

pages.title: Application to finite and quasi-finite morphisms
pages.html:
6 Application to finite and quasi-finite morphisms

Cf. EGA IV 18.12 for generalizations to preschemes not necessarily locally Noetherian

We shall prove the two following theorems:

Theorem 1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism with finite fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Then \(f\) is finite.

Theorem 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and separated morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Then \(f\) is quasi-affine, and a fortiori quasi-projective.

Remarks 3

Theorem 1 is well known, and due to Chevalley in the case of algebraic varieties; one will also find a simple proof of it in [EGA III 4], using the “theorem of holomorphic functions”. The proof given here does not use this last theorem, but on the other hand descent theory; we give it as a bonus to the reader, since one has it “for free” at the same time as that of 2. Let us also recall ([EGA III 4] or [1]) that the global form of Zariski's “Main Theorem”, deduced from the “theorem of holomorphic functions”, asserts that if \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is quasi-finite and quasi-projective, \(Y\) being Noetherian, then \(X\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to an open subprescheme of a finite \(Y\)-prescheme \(Z\). The conjunction of the “Main Theorem” and of 2 is thus stated as follows:

Corollary 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and separated morphism, with \(Y\) Noetherian. Then \(X\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to an open subprescheme of a finite \(Y\)-prescheme \(Z\).

Another interesting consequence of 2 for descent theory will be given with 9.

Proof of 1 and 2

We shall admit the following fact, whose proof is easy1:

Lemma 5

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and let \(y\in Y\). For there to exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) is finite (resp. quasi-affine, resp. ...) over \(U\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\times_{Y}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{y})\) be finite (resp. quasi-affine, resp. ...) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{y})\).

Since, on the other hand, the property for \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of being finite, resp. quasi-affine, is local on \(Y\), we are reduced, in order to prove 1 and 2, to the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of a local ring, and is a fortiori of finite dimension. (N.B. one calls dimension of a prescheme \(Y\) the sup of the Krull dimensions of its local rings). We proceed by induction on

\[ n=\dim(Y), \]

the assertion being trivial for \(n<0\). We may therefore suppose \(n\geqslant 0\), and the assertion proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). One may again suppose that \(Y\) is the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring \(A\), of dimension \(n\). Let us note that the hypotheses made in 1 and 2 are stable under base change (we have already used this in the reduction at the beginning), they will remain true after the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Since this last is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, the statements 7 and 9 reduce us to the case where \(A\) is moreover complete. Then, using the fact that every local Noetherian ring \(B\) over \(A\) quasi-finite over \(A\) is finite over \(A\), and the fact that \(X\) is separated over \(Y\) and the fibre of \(y\) is formed of isolated points, one finds a decomposition

\[ X=X'\amalg X'' \]

where \(X'\) is finite over \(Y\), and where the fibre of \(X''\) at \(y\) is empty. If \(X\) is proper over \(Y\), the same holds for \(X''\), so its image in \(Y\) is closed, and since it does not contain \(y\), it is empty, so \(X''=\emptyset\) so \(X=X'\), which shows that \(X\) is finite over \(Y\) and proves 1 (N.B. the induction hypothesis is here useless). If \(X\) is quasi-finite over \(Y\), \(X''\) is so too, but \(X''\) in fact lies over the open set \(Y-\{ y\}\) of \(Y\), which is of dimension \(<n\). By virtue of the induction hypothesis, \(X''\) is quasi-affine over \(Y-\{ y\}\), hence also over \(Y\), the same evidently holds for \(X'\), hence also for their sum \(X\), which proves 2.

Remark 6

Theorems 1 and 2 remain valid if one no longer supposes \(Y\) locally Noetherian, on condition of specifying that one supposes \(f\) of finite presentation (cf. 6). Indeed, one may again suppose \(Y\) affine, and then one verifies without difficulty that the situation \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is deduced, by a base change \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_{0}\), from a situation \(f_{0}\colon X_{0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_{0}\) satisfying the same hypotheses as \(f\), with \(Y_{0}\) Noetherian. So by the result 1 resp. 2, \(f_{0}\) is finite resp. quasi-affine, so the same holds for \(f\). This kind of reasoning is often useful for getting rid of Noetherian hypotheses, (which always end up being troublesome in the applications).

blocks.html: 6 Application to finite and quasi-finite morphisms
Cf. EGA IV 18.12 for generalizations to preschemes not necessarily locally Noetherian
We shall prove the two following theorems:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism with finite fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Then \(f\) is finite.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and separated morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Then \(f\) is quasi-affine, and a fortiori quasi-projective.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3

Theorem 1 is well known, and due to Chevalley in the case of algebraic varieties; one will also find a simple proof of it in [EGA III 4], using the “theorem of holomorphic functions”. The proof given here does not use this last theorem, but on the other hand descent theory; we give it as a bonus to the reader, since one has it “for free” at the same time as that of 2. Let us also recall ([EGA III 4] or [1]) that the global form of Zariski's “Main Theorem”, deduced from the “theorem of holomorphic functions”, asserts that if \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is quasi-finite and quasi-projective, \(Y\) being Noetherian, then \(X\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to an open subprescheme of a finite \(Y\)-prescheme \(Z\). The conjunction of the “Main Theorem” and of 2 is thus stated as follows:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and separated morphism, with \(Y\) Noetherian. Then \(X\) is \(Y\)-isomorphic to an open subprescheme of a finite \(Y\)-prescheme \(Z\).
Another interesting consequence of 2 for descent theory will be given with 9.
Proof of 1 and 2
We shall admit the following fact, whose proof is easy1:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type over \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and let \(y\in Y\). For there to exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) is finite (resp. quasi-affine, resp. ...) over \(U\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\times_{Y}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{y})\) be finite (resp. quasi-affine, resp. ...) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{y})\).

Since, on the other hand, the property for \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of being finite, resp. quasi-affine, is local on \(Y\), we are reduced, in order to prove 1 and 2, to the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of a local ring, and is a fortiori of finite dimension. (N.B. one calls dimension of a prescheme \(Y\) the sup of the Krull dimensions of its local rings). We proceed by induction on
\[ n=\dim(Y), \]

the assertion being trivial for \(n<0\). We may therefore suppose \(n\geqslant 0\), and the assertion proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). One may again suppose that \(Y\) is the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring \(A\), of dimension \(n\). Let us note that the hypotheses made in 1 and 2 are stable under base change (we have already used this in the reduction at the beginning), they will remain true after the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{A})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Since this last is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, the statements 7 and 9 reduce us to the case where \(A\) is moreover complete. Then, using the fact that every local Noetherian ring \(B\) over \(A\) quasi-finite over \(A\) is finite over \(A\), and the fact that \(X\) is separated over \(Y\) and the fibre of \(y\) is formed of isolated points, one finds a decomposition
\[ X=X'\amalg X'' \]

where \(X'\) is finite over \(Y\), and where the fibre of \(X''\) at \(y\) is empty. If \(X\) is proper over \(Y\), the same holds for \(X''\), so its image in \(Y\) is closed, and since it does not contain \(y\), it is empty, so \(X''=\emptyset\) so \(X=X'\), which shows that \(X\) is finite over \(Y\) and proves 1 (N.B. the induction hypothesis is here useless). If \(X\) is quasi-finite over \(Y\), \(X''\) is so too, but \(X''\) in fact lies over the open set \(Y-\{ y\}\) of \(Y\), which is of dimension \(<n\). By virtue of the induction hypothesis, \(X''\) is quasi-affine over \(Y-\{ y\}\), hence also over \(Y\), the same evidently holds for \(X'\), hence also for their sum \(X\), which proves 2.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6

Theorems 1 and 2 remain valid if one no longer supposes \(Y\) locally Noetherian, on condition of specifying that one supposes \(f\) of finite presentation (cf. 6). Indeed, one may again suppose \(Y\) affine, and then one verifies without difficulty that the situation \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is deduced, by a base change \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_{0}\), from a situation \(f_{0}\colon X_{0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_{0}\) satisfying the same hypotheses as \(f\), with \(Y_{0}\) Noetherian. So by the result 1 resp. 2, \(f_{0}\) is finite resp. quasi-affine, so the same holds for \(f\). This kind of reasoning is often useful for getting rid of Noetherian hypotheses, (which always end up being troublesome in the applications).

pages.title: Effectivity criteria for a descent datum
pages.html:
7 Effectivity criteria for a descent datum

Let us consider, as usual, a morphism of preschemes

\[ g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

and an \(S'\)-prescheme \(X'\). In accordance with the general facts VII, 91, the datum of a descent datum on \(X'\), relative to \(g\), is equivalent to the datum of an equivalence couple [3]:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{q_{1},q_{2}\colon X''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{X'}} \]

such that the structural morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is compatible with this couple and the equivalence couple

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{p_{1},p_{2}\colon S''=S'\times_{S}S'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}} \]

defined by \(g\), and such that the two squares (or one of the two, which amounts to the same by reason of symmetry) extracted from the corresponding diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[d]&{X''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]\ar[d]\\{S'}&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]} \]

(using either \(p_{1},q_{1}\), or \(p_{2},q_{2}\)), be cartesian. A solution of the descent problem posed by this descent datum, i.e. an object \(X\) over \(S\) equipped with an isomorphism \(X\times_{S}S'\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X'\) compatible with the descent data, is equivalent to the datum of a cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[l]_{h}\ar[d]\\{S}&{S'}\ar[l]_{g}} \]

satisfying \(hq_{1}=hq_{2}\).

Since the set of faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms is stable under base change, and since a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism is an effective epimorphism by virtue of 3, it follows from the general theory [D]:

Proposition 1

Suppose \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact. For a descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the equivalence relation \(R=(q_{1},q_{2})\) that it defines be effective (i.e. the quotient \(X'/R\) exists and \(X''\) becomes the fibred square of \(X'\) over \(X'/R\)), and that the canonical morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'/R\) be faithfully flat and quasi-compact.

Thus the question of the effectivity of a descent datum is a particular case of the question of effectivity of an equivalence graph, and various effectivity criteria given in this section can be obtained in this way. Nevertheless, in the context of descent one has at one's disposal theorem 1, which implies that if \(X'\) is affine over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, a statement which has no analogue for passage to the quotient by a general flat equivalence graph. All the effectivity criteria that we give here can also be regarded as deduced from the preceding one.

Let \(U'\) be a sub-prescheme of \(X'\) (or more generally a subobject of \(X'\) in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}\)); one says that \(U'\) is stable under the descent datum on \(X'\), if one can find on \(U'\) a descent datum relative to \(g\), such that the immersion \(U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is compatible with the descent data. This also means that the inverse images of \(U'\) in \(X''\) by \(q_{1}\) and \(q_{2}\) are the same (or also, as one says, that \(U'\) is stable under the equivalence relation \(R\)), and of course the descent datum in question on \(U'\) is then unique, and called the descent datum induced by that of \(X'\). This being so:

Proposition 2

Let \((X_{i}')\) be a covering of \(X'\) by opens \(X_{i}'\) stable under the descent datum. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for the descent data induced in the \(X_{i}'\).

This is an easy consequence of 1 for example, and the detail of the proof is left to the reader.

Corollary 3

Let \((S_{i})\) be an open covering of \(S\), and for every \(i\) let \(S_{i}'\) and \(X_{i}'\) be deduced from \(S'\) and \(X'\) by the base change \(S_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(i\), the descent datum on \(X_{i}'\) relative to \(g_{i}\colon S_{i}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_{i}\) be effective.

This criterion always brings us back in practice to the case where \(S\) is affine. In the case where \(S'\) is likewise affine, which is the most frequent case in the applications, one has:

Corollary 4

Suppose \(S\) and \(S'\) affine. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be a union of opens \(X_{i}'\) affine and stable under the descent datum.

The sufficiency comes from 2 and from the fact that if \(X_{i}'\) is affine, it is affine over \(S'\) and one can apply 1. For the necessity, one notes that if \(X'\) comes from \(X\), and if \(X\) is covered by affine opens \(X_{i}\), then the \(X_{i}'=X_{i}\times_{S}S'\) are affine opens stable under the descent data and covering \(X'\).

Corollary 5

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat, quasi-compact and radicial morphism. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent, i.e. for every \(X'\) over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is effective.

Indeed, by virtue of 3 one may suppose \(S\) affine, hence since \(S'\) is radicial over \(S\) and therefore separated, \(S'\) is separated. Moreover, for every \(x'\in X'\), the fibre \(R(x')=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(x'))\) of the set-theoretic equivalence relation defined by the equivalence relation \(R\) is reduced to a single point, for \(g\) being radicial, the same holds of \(p_{1},p_{2}\) which are deduced from it by the base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), hence also of \(q_{1},q_{2}\) which are deduced from the preceding ones by the base change \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S''\). Hence every open of \(X'\) is stable under the descent datum. Let us then cover \(X'\) by affine opens \(X_{i}'\); they are affine over \(S\) since \(S'\) is separated, hence the induced descent datum is effective by 1. One then concludes by 2.

One will note that 5 gives the only known case of a morphism of effective descent in the category of preschemes, and it is probably indeed the only case, even limiting oneself to Noetherian schemes, or to schemes of finite type over a field.

When one supposes \(S\) locally Noetherian and \(S'\) of finite type over \(S\), statement 5 is also a particular case of the following one (which generalizes Weil's Galois descent and Cartier's inseparable descent):

Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite locally free (i.e. defined by an Algebra over \(S\) which is a locally free module of finite type) and surjective morphism (hence \(g\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, hence a morphism of descent). Let \(X'\) be an \(S'\)-prescheme equipped with a descent datum. For this datum to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(x'\in X'\), the fibre \(R(x')=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(x'))\) be contained in an affine open (a condition automatically satisfied if \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\)).

The remark in parentheses comes from the fact that if \(s\) is the point of \(S\) below \(x'\), then \(R(x')\) is finite and contained in the fibre \(X'_{s}\), on the other hand since \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\) and \(S'\) is finite over \(S\), \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S\), which implies that a fibre of \(X'\) over \(S\) is contained in an affine open.

Since every finite part of an affine scheme admits a fundamental system of affine neighbourhoods, one sees that one does not lose the hypothesis by restricting oneself over an affine open of \(S\), which by virtue of 3 reduces us to the case where \(S\) is affine. By virtue of 4, we are reduced to showing that \(x'\) is contained in an affine open stable under the descent datum. Let indeed \(U\) be an affine open containing \(R(x')\); then the saturation

\[ R(X'-U)=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(X'-U)) \]

does not meet \(R(x')\); on the other hand, since \(q_{2}\) is finite (for \(g\) hence \(p_{2}\) is so) hence closed, the second member is a closed part of \(X'\). Let \(U'\) be its complement in \(X'\); it is thus a saturated open and we have

\[ R(x')\subset U'\subset U \]

with \(U\) affine, but \(U'\) not affine a priori. Since a finite part \(R(x')\) in an affine scheme \(U\) has a fundamental system of affine neighbourhoods of the form \(U_{f}\), one sees, replacing \(f\) by its restriction to \(U'\), that there exists a section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{U}\) such that:

\[ R(x')\subset U_{f}',\quad U_{f}'\textrm{ is affine}. \]

Let then \(U''=q_{1}^{-1}(U')=q_{2}^{-1}(U')\); let us again denote by \(q_{1},q_{2}\) the induced morphisms \(U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U'\), and consider

\[ f'=\operatorname{N}_{q_{2}}(q_{1}^*(f)), \]

where \(\operatorname{N}_{q_{2}}\) denotes the norm relatively to the finite locally free morphism \(q_{2}\colon U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U'\). The compatibility of the formation of the norm with base change easily implies that \(f'\) is an invariant section:

\[ q_{1}^*(f')=q_{2}^*(f') \]

which implies that \(U_{f'}'\) is a saturated open of \(U'\). More precisely moreover, denoting by \(Z(f')\) the set of zeros of a section \(f'\), one finds by virtue of the properties of norms:

\[ Z(f')=q_{2}(Z(q_{1}^*(f)))=q_{2}(q_{1}^*(Z(f)))= R(U'-U_{f}'), \]

which implies that \(U_{f'}'=U'-Z(f')\) is saturated, contains \(R(x')\), and is contained in \(U_{f}'\). Since the latter is affine, it follows that \(U_{f'}'\) is so too (for it is equal to \((U_{f}')_{f''}\), with \(f''=f'|U_{f'}'\)). It is thus a saturated affine open containing \(R(x')\) hence \(x'\), which completes the proof.

One will note that this reasoning applies whenever one has an equivalence relation (or even merely a pre-equivalence relation, cf. [3]) in a prescheme \(X'\), finite and locally free, and moreover 6 is also a particular case of the analogous result for finite and locally free pre-equivalences, cf. loc. cit. The same remark holds for 7 below.

One can also, once the existence of a saturated quasi-affine open \(U'\) containing \(x'\) has been obtained, appeal to 9 and 2, which avoids resorting to norms.

Let us note moreover that under the conditions of 6, if the descent datum on \(X'\) is effective, \(X'\) coming from \(X\) over \(S\), then the morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is finite, locally free and surjective, for it is deduced from \(g\) by the base change \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). It follows (EGA II 6.6.4) that if \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\) hence over \(S\), then \(X\) is quasi-projective over \(S\), (an invertible sheaf relatively ample on \(X\) being obtained by taking the norm of an invertible sheaf on \(X'\) relatively ample over \(S\), or over \(S'\), it amounts to the same). One thus obtains:

Corollary 7

A finite locally free and surjective morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes quasi-projective over others, i.e. for every \(X'\) quasi-projective over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, and the descended \(S\)-prescheme \(X\) is quasi-projective over \(S\).

Proposition 8

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes \(Z\) quasi-compact over a prescheme \(T\), equipped with an invertible sheaf ample relatively to \(T\). In particular, for every prescheme \(X'\) over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and every invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{L}'\) on \(X'\) ample relatively to \(S'\), equipped also with a descent datum relative to the one given on \(X'\), (i.e. equipped with an isomorphism of \(q_{1}^*(\mathscr{L}')\) with \(q_{2}^*(\mathscr{L}')\), satisfying the usual transitivity condition), the descent datum on \(X'\) is effective, and the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{L}\) on the descended prescheme \(X\), deduced from \(\mathscr{L}'\) by descent, is ample relatively to \(S\).

The proof is entirely analogous to that of 8, noting that on the quasi-coherent graded Algebra \(\mathscr{S}'\) on \(S'\) defined by \(\mathscr{L}'\), there is a descent datum, allowing one to construct a quasi-coherent graded Algebra \(\mathscr{S}\) on \(S\) thanks to 1, whence a \(P=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\) on \(S\) such that \(P'=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}')\) is identified, with its descent datum, with \(P\times_{S}S'\). Since by hypothesis \(X'\) is identified with an open of \(P'\), necessarily stable under the descent datum on \(P'\), the descent datum on \(X'\) is likewise effective, and one obtains the descended prescheme as an open in \(P\). The details are left to the reader. — In particular, taking \(\mathscr{L}'=\mathscr{O}_{X'}\), one finds:

Corollary 9

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, and let \(X'\) be a quasi-affine prescheme over \(S'\); then every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, and the descended prescheme \(X\) is quasi-affine over \(S\).

By virtue of 2, this result applies in particular if \(S'\) is locally Noetherian and \(X'\) is quasi-finite and separated over \(S'\), more generally if \(S'\) is arbitrary and \(X'\) is of finite presentation, quasi-finite and separated over \(S'\) (cf. 6).

Remarks 10

The results given in this number exhaust the criteria of effectivity currently known, and probably even the useful criteria that exist2. One will note the following counterexamples in support of this assertion:

  1. (i)

    If \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, and \(S'\) is the spectrum of a quadratic Galois extension, one can find an \(X'\) over \(S'\) proper and smooth over \(S'\), of dimension 3, endowed with a descent datum that is not effective (Serre).

  2. (ii)

    One can find an \(S\) spectrum of a regular local ring of dimension 3 (if one wishes, the local ring of an algebraic scheme over a field of given characteristic), a \(T\) principal covering of \(S\) with group \(\mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}\), such that, if \(t\) denotes one of the points of \(T\) above the closed point \(s\) of \(S\), and \(S'=T-s\), one can find an \(X'\) projective over \(S'\), regular, endowed with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), this descent datum not being effective.

For these constructions one uses Hironaka's example of non-projective varieties. For (i), it suffices to use the fact that one can find over \(k\) a proper and smooth scheme \(X_{0}\) of dimension 3, on which \(G=\mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}\) acts without inertia, and in which there exist two points \(a,b\) rational over \(S\), congruent under \(G\), which are not contained in an affine open set. One then sets \(X'=X_{0}\times_{k}k'\), one makes \(G\) act on \(X'\) by means of the operations of \(G\) on the two factors, which gives a descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon \operatorname{Spec}(k')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(k)\). Above \(a\) resp. \(b\), there is exactly one point \(a'\) resp. \(b'\), (up to quadratic residual extension), and \(a'\) and \(b'\) are congruent under \(G\), since \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{0}\) is compatible with the operations of \(G\). Then \(a'\) and \(b'\) cannot be contained in an affine open set, say \(U'\), for then \(U=X_{0}-\operatorname{Im}(X'-U')\) would be an open set of \(X_{0}\) containing \((a,b)\) and whose inverse image in \(X'\) would be contained in \(U'\), hence quasi-affine, hence \(U\) would be quasi-affine, and consequently \((a,b)\) would have an affine neighbourhood in \(U\).

For (ii), one uses the fact that in Hironaka's example, \(X_{0}\) is obtained as a proper prescheme above a projective \(k\)-scheme \(Y\), smooth over \(k\) (the morphism \(f\colon X_{0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being moreover birational, but this matters little), the group \(G\) acting likewise on \(Y\) in a manner compatible with its operations on \(X_{0}\), and finally setting \(S'=Y-f(b)\), \(X'=X_{0}|S'\), \(X'\) is projective over \(S'\). Then \(X_{0}\) is endowed with a natural descent datum relative to the canonical morphism \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S=Y/G\), by means of the operations of \(G\) on \(X_{0}\) compatible with its operations on \(Y\). This descent datum is not effective, since \((a,b)\) is not contained in an affine open set. The descent datum induced on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is then not effective, as one verifies easily.

[D]

blocks.html: 7 Effectivity criteria for a descent datum
Let us consider, as usual, a morphism of preschemes
\[ g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]
and an \(S'\)-prescheme \(X'\). In accordance with the general facts VII, 91, the datum of a descent datum on \(X'\), relative to \(g\), is equivalent to the datum of an equivalence couple [3]:
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{q_{1},q_{2}\colon X''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{X'}} \]
such that the structural morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is compatible with this couple and the equivalence couple
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{p_{1},p_{2}\colon S''=S'\times_{S}S'}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}} \]
defined by \(g\), and such that the two squares (or one of the two, which amounts to the same by reason of symmetry) extracted from the corresponding diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[d]&{X''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]\ar[d]\\{S'}&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[l]\ar@<-2pt>[l]} \]

(using either \(p_{1},q_{1}\), or \(p_{2},q_{2}\)), be cartesian. A solution of the descent problem posed by this descent datum, i.e. an object \(X\) over \(S\) equipped with an isomorphism \(X\times_{S}S'\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X'\) compatible with the descent data, is equivalent to the datum of a cartesian square
\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[l]_{h}\ar[d]\\{S}&{S'}\ar[l]_{g}} \]
satisfying \(hq_{1}=hq_{2}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Suppose \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact. For a descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the equivalence relation \(R=(q_{1},q_{2})\) that it defines be effective (i.e. the quotient \(X'/R\) exists and \(X''\) becomes the fibred square of \(X'\) over \(X'/R\)), and that the canonical morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'/R\) be faithfully flat and quasi-compact.

Thus the question of the effectivity of a descent datum is a particular case of the question of effectivity of an equivalence graph, and various effectivity criteria given in this section can be obtained in this way. Nevertheless, in the context of descent one has at one's disposal theorem 1, which implies that if \(X'\) is affine over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, a statement which has no analogue for passage to the quotient by a general flat equivalence graph. All the effectivity criteria that we give here can also be regarded as deduced from the preceding one.

Let \(U'\) be a sub-prescheme of \(X'\) (or more generally a subobject of \(X'\) in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}\)); one says that \(U'\) is stable under the descent datum on \(X'\), if one can find on \(U'\) a descent datum relative to \(g\), such that the immersion \(U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is compatible with the descent data. This also means that the inverse images of \(U'\) in \(X''\) by \(q_{1}\) and \(q_{2}\) are the same (or also, as one says, that \(U'\) is stable under the equivalence relation \(R\)), and of course the descent datum in question on \(U'\) is then unique, and called the descent datum induced by that of \(X'\). This being so:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \((X_{i}')\) be a covering of \(X'\) by opens \(X_{i}'\) stable under the descent datum. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for the descent data induced in the \(X_{i}'\).
This is an easy consequence of 1 for example, and the detail of the proof is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \((S_{i})\) be an open covering of \(S\), and for every \(i\) let \(S_{i}'\) and \(X_{i}'\) be deduced from \(S'\) and \(X'\) by the base change \(S_{i}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(i\), the descent datum on \(X_{i}'\) relative to \(g_{i}\colon S_{i}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_{i}\) be effective.
This criterion always brings us back in practice to the case where \(S\) is affine. In the case where \(S'\) is likewise affine, which is the most frequent case in the applications, one has:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Suppose \(S\) and \(S'\) affine. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be a union of opens \(X_{i}'\) affine and stable under the descent datum.

The sufficiency comes from 2 and from the fact that if \(X_{i}'\) is affine, it is affine over \(S'\) and one can apply 1. For the necessity, one notes that if \(X'\) comes from \(X\), and if \(X\) is covered by affine opens \(X_{i}\), then the \(X_{i}'=X_{i}\times_{S}S'\) are affine opens stable under the descent data and covering \(X'\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat, quasi-compact and radicial morphism. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent, i.e. for every \(X'\) over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is effective.

Indeed, by virtue of 3 one may suppose \(S\) affine, hence since \(S'\) is radicial over \(S\) and therefore separated, \(S'\) is separated. Moreover, for every \(x'\in X'\), the fibre \(R(x')=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(x'))\) of the set-theoretic equivalence relation defined by the equivalence relation \(R\) is reduced to a single point, for \(g\) being radicial, the same holds of \(p_{1},p_{2}\) which are deduced from it by the base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), hence also of \(q_{1},q_{2}\) which are deduced from the preceding ones by the base change \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S''\). Hence every open of \(X'\) is stable under the descent datum. Let us then cover \(X'\) by affine opens \(X_{i}'\); they are affine over \(S\) since \(S'\) is separated, hence the induced descent datum is effective by 1. One then concludes by 2.

One will note that 5 gives the only known case of a morphism of effective descent in the category of preschemes, and it is probably indeed the only case, even limiting oneself to Noetherian schemes, or to schemes of finite type over a field.

When one supposes \(S\) locally Noetherian and \(S'\) of finite type over \(S\), statement 5 is also a particular case of the following one (which generalizes Weil's Galois descent and Cartier's inseparable descent):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite locally free (i.e. defined by an Algebra over \(S\) which is a locally free module of finite type) and surjective morphism (hence \(g\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, hence a morphism of descent). Let \(X'\) be an \(S'\)-prescheme equipped with a descent datum. For this datum to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(x'\in X'\), the fibre \(R(x')=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(x'))\) be contained in an affine open (a condition automatically satisfied if \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\)).

The remark in parentheses comes from the fact that if \(s\) is the point of \(S\) below \(x'\), then \(R(x')\) is finite and contained in the fibre \(X'_{s}\), on the other hand since \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\) and \(S'\) is finite over \(S\), \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S\), which implies that a fibre of \(X'\) over \(S\) is contained in an affine open.

Since every finite part of an affine scheme admits a fundamental system of affine neighbourhoods, one sees that one does not lose the hypothesis by restricting oneself over an affine open of \(S\), which by virtue of 3 reduces us to the case where \(S\) is affine. By virtue of 4, we are reduced to showing that \(x'\) is contained in an affine open stable under the descent datum. Let indeed \(U\) be an affine open containing \(R(x')\); then the saturation
\[ R(X'-U)=q_{2}(q_{1}^{-1}(X'-U)) \]

does not meet \(R(x')\); on the other hand, since \(q_{2}\) is finite (for \(g\) hence \(p_{2}\) is so) hence closed, the second member is a closed part of \(X'\). Let \(U'\) be its complement in \(X'\); it is thus a saturated open and we have
\[ R(x')\subset U'\subset U \]

with \(U\) affine, but \(U'\) not affine a priori. Since a finite part \(R(x')\) in an affine scheme \(U\) has a fundamental system of affine neighbourhoods of the form \(U_{f}\), one sees, replacing \(f\) by its restriction to \(U'\), that there exists a section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{U}\) such that:
\[ R(x')\subset U_{f}',\quad U_{f}'\textrm{ is affine}. \]

Let then \(U''=q_{1}^{-1}(U')=q_{2}^{-1}(U')\); let us again denote by \(q_{1},q_{2}\) the induced morphisms \(U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U'\), and consider
\[ f'=\operatorname{N}_{q_{2}}(q_{1}^*(f)), \]

where \(\operatorname{N}_{q_{2}}\) denotes the norm relatively to the finite locally free morphism \(q_{2}\colon U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U'\). The compatibility of the formation of the norm with base change easily implies that \(f'\) is an invariant section:
\[ q_{1}^*(f')=q_{2}^*(f') \]
which implies that \(U_{f'}'\) is a saturated open of \(U'\). More precisely moreover, denoting by \(Z(f')\) the set of zeros of a section \(f'\), one finds by virtue of the properties of norms:
\[ Z(f')=q_{2}(Z(q_{1}^*(f)))=q_{2}(q_{1}^*(Z(f)))= R(U'-U_{f}'), \]

which implies that \(U_{f'}'=U'-Z(f')\) is saturated, contains \(R(x')\), and is contained in \(U_{f}'\). Since the latter is affine, it follows that \(U_{f'}'\) is so too (for it is equal to \((U_{f}')_{f''}\), with \(f''=f'|U_{f'}'\)). It is thus a saturated affine open containing \(R(x')\) hence \(x'\), which completes the proof.

One will note that this reasoning applies whenever one has an equivalence relation (or even merely a pre-equivalence relation, cf. [3]) in a prescheme \(X'\), finite and locally free, and moreover 6 is also a particular case of the analogous result for finite and locally free pre-equivalences, cf. loc. cit. The same remark holds for 7 below.
One can also, once the existence of a saturated quasi-affine open \(U'\) containing \(x'\) has been obtained, appeal to 9 and 2, which avoids resorting to norms.

Let us note moreover that under the conditions of 6, if the descent datum on \(X'\) is effective, \(X'\) coming from \(X\) over \(S\), then the morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is finite, locally free and surjective, for it is deduced from \(g\) by the base change \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). It follows (EGA II 6.6.4) that if \(X'\) is quasi-projective over \(S'\) hence over \(S\), then \(X\) is quasi-projective over \(S\), (an invertible sheaf relatively ample on \(X\) being obtained by taking the norm of an invertible sheaf on \(X'\) relatively ample over \(S\), or over \(S'\), it amounts to the same). One thus obtains:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

A finite locally free and surjective morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes quasi-projective over others, i.e. for every \(X'\) quasi-projective over \(S'\), every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, and the descended \(S\)-prescheme \(X\) is quasi-projective over \(S\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes \(Z\) quasi-compact over a prescheme \(T\), equipped with an invertible sheaf ample relatively to \(T\). In particular, for every prescheme \(X'\) over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and every invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{L}'\) on \(X'\) ample relatively to \(S'\), equipped also with a descent datum relative to the one given on \(X'\), (i.e. equipped with an isomorphism of \(q_{1}^*(\mathscr{L}')\) with \(q_{2}^*(\mathscr{L}')\), satisfying the usual transitivity condition), the descent datum on \(X'\) is effective, and the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{L}\) on the descended prescheme \(X\), deduced from \(\mathscr{L}'\) by descent, is ample relatively to \(S\).

The proof is entirely analogous to that of 8, noting that on the quasi-coherent graded Algebra \(\mathscr{S}'\) on \(S'\) defined by \(\mathscr{L}'\), there is a descent datum, allowing one to construct a quasi-coherent graded Algebra \(\mathscr{S}\) on \(S\) thanks to 1, whence a \(P=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S})\) on \(S\) such that \(P'=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathscr{S}')\) is identified, with its descent datum, with \(P\times_{S}S'\). Since by hypothesis \(X'\) is identified with an open of \(P'\), necessarily stable under the descent datum on \(P'\), the descent datum on \(X'\) is likewise effective, and one obtains the descended prescheme as an open in \(P\). The details are left to the reader. — In particular, taking \(\mathscr{L}'=\mathscr{O}_{X'}\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, and let \(X'\) be a quasi-affine prescheme over \(S'\); then every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\) is effective, and the descended prescheme \(X\) is quasi-affine over \(S\).

By virtue of 2, this result applies in particular if \(S'\) is locally Noetherian and \(X'\) is quasi-finite and separated over \(S'\), more generally if \(S'\) is arbitrary and \(X'\) is of finite presentation, quasi-finite and separated over \(S'\) (cf. 6).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 10

The results given in this number exhaust the criteria of effectivity currently known, and probably even the useful criteria that exist2. One will note the following counterexamples in support of this assertion:

  1. (i)

    If \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, and \(S'\) is the spectrum of a quadratic Galois extension, one can find an \(X'\) over \(S'\) proper and smooth over \(S'\), of dimension 3, endowed with a descent datum that is not effective (Serre).

  2. (ii)

    One can find an \(S\) spectrum of a regular local ring of dimension 3 (if one wishes, the local ring of an algebraic scheme over a field of given characteristic), a \(T\) principal covering of \(S\) with group \(\mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}\), such that, if \(t\) denotes one of the points of \(T\) above the closed point \(s\) of \(S\), and \(S'=T-s\), one can find an \(X'\) projective over \(S'\), regular, endowed with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), this descent datum not being effective.

For these constructions one uses Hironaka's example of non-projective varieties. For (i), it suffices to use the fact that one can find over \(k\) a proper and smooth scheme \(X_{0}\) of dimension 3, on which \(G=\mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}\) acts without inertia, and in which there exist two points \(a,b\) rational over \(S\), congruent under \(G\), which are not contained in an affine open set. One then sets \(X'=X_{0}\times_{k}k'\), one makes \(G\) act on \(X'\) by means of the operations of \(G\) on the two factors, which gives a descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon \operatorname{Spec}(k')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(k)\). Above \(a\) resp. \(b\), there is exactly one point \(a'\) resp. \(b'\), (up to quadratic residual extension), and \(a'\) and \(b'\) are congruent under \(G\), since \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{0}\) is compatible with the operations of \(G\). Then \(a'\) and \(b'\) cannot be contained in an affine open set, say \(U'\), for then \(U=X_{0}-\operatorname{Im}(X'-U')\) would be an open set of \(X_{0}\) containing \((a,b)\) and whose inverse image in \(X'\) would be contained in \(U'\), hence quasi-affine, hence \(U\) would be quasi-affine, and consequently \((a,b)\) would have an affine neighbourhood in \(U\).

For (ii), one uses the fact that in Hironaka's example, \(X_{0}\) is obtained as a proper prescheme above a projective \(k\)-scheme \(Y\), smooth over \(k\) (the morphism \(f\colon X_{0}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being moreover birational, but this matters little), the group \(G\) acting likewise on \(Y\) in a manner compatible with its operations on \(X_{0}\), and finally setting \(S'=Y-f(b)\), \(X'=X_{0}|S'\), \(X'\) is projective over \(S'\). Then \(X_{0}\) is endowed with a natural descent datum relative to the canonical morphism \(Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S=Y/G\), by means of the operations of \(G\) on \(X_{0}\) compatible with its operations on \(Y\). This descent datum is not effective, since \((a,b)\) is not contained in an affine open set. The descent datum induced on \(X'\) relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is then not effective, as one verifies easily.
